sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 4-year-old's mother, presents with a history of a skiing accident in Vermont last weekend, where she fell and her knee popped, resulting in pain and inability to bear weight.
She was taken down the hill by the ski patrol.
The patient reports pain on the outside of the knee, which feels like it won't hold her weight, and is unable to bend or straighten the knee fully.
She denies numbness or tingling in her toes and is generally healthy, with no high blood pressure, diabetes, or other medical conditions.
The patient is taking ibuprofen for pain, which provides some relief.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: [NOT MENTIONED]
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension of the right knee.
The patient has a positive Lachman sign, palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse, and no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in the right knee, based on the mechanism of injury and physical exam findings.
Problem list: ACL injury; status: suspected; knee pain; status: active; knee instability; status: suspected
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain management.
I advised the patient to continue using a brace to support the knee.
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the suspected ACL injury and assess for any other potential damage.
I will review the results with the patient in the next few days and discuss further management.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again soon to review the MRI results and discuss the next steps in management.
